reason:
PROPER-TIME CLAIM WITHOUT NORMALIZATION: Definition 1 (line 57-58) states 'We take tau to be the proper-time parameter' but only requires eta_{ab} dot-gamma^a dot-gamma^b < 0, NOT the proper-time normalization eta_{ab} dot-gamma^a dot-gamma^b = -1. These are different conditions. With tau as proper time, we must have eta_{ab} dot-gamma^a dot-gamma^b = -c^2 (or -1 in natural units). The definition only requires strict negativity, which allows arbitrary timelike parametrisations (e.g., affine but non-unit-speed).

This is not just a pedantic distinction -- it directly impacts the properness argument. With the normalization = -1, we get (dot-gamma^0)^2 = 1 + sum(dot-gamma^i)^2 >= 1, hence dot-gamma^0 >= 1 (using future-directedness), which forces gamma^0(tau) >= tau + const -> +infinity, establishing surjectivity of gamma^0 and making the properness proof work. Without the normalization, gamma^0 can grow arbitrarily slowly (see the arctan counterexample in ch-24c2ca76cf26849c).

The four-momentum definition p^alpha = m dot-gamma^alpha (line 67) also implicitly assumes proper-time parametrisation, since the physical four-momentum requires p^alpha p_alpha = -m^2, which holds iff eta_{ab} dot-gamma^a dot-gamma^b = -1.

Fix: Either add 'eta_{ab} dot-gamma^a dot-gamma^b = -1' as an explicit condition in Definition 1, or change 'tau is the proper-time parameter' to 'tau is an arbitrary parameter along the curve' and add the normalization separately where needed.
